title: Shell completion
body:
Add shell completion with cobra
 
Allow automatic generation for shell completion scripts
with the internal cobra functions (requires v1.0.0)
  
This should replace the handwritten completion scripts
and even adds support for fish and powershell(if needed?)

We can now create the scripts with
- podman completion bash
- podman completion zsh
- podman completion fish
- podman completion powershell

to test the completion run:
`source <(podman completion bash)`

I added the main completion for all commands
but this still could be improved with
better custom completion functions.
see: cmd/podman/completion/completion.go

Flag completion is for now only added to
podman generate systemd --restart-policy
for now
just to show how to implement this on flags

This is still **WIP**

Closes #6440

comments.body:
Hi @Luap99. Thanks for your PR.

I'm waiting for a [containers](https://github.com/orgs/containers/people) member to verify that this patch is reasonable to test. If it is, they should reply with `/ok-to-test` on its own line. Until that is done, I will not automatically test new commits in this PR, but the usual testing commands by org members will still work. Regular contributors should [join the org](https://github.com/orgs/containers/people) to skip this step.

Once the patch is verified, the new status will be reflected by the `ok-to-test` label.

I understand the commands that are listed [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod).

<details>

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>
/hold
@rhatdan @TomSweeneyRedHat please take a look :)

Ok i played with this a bit and tested the bash, zsh and fish completions. Bash and fish are working fine but zsh is not working properly (only completes static flags). There is already a [PR](https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1070) in the cobra project which fixes this Issue so this should work once this get merged.

I start adding autocompletion support for the flags

@edsantiago PTAL  I know you have played in this area in the past.

I started adding completion for flags. I created the table below so you can see my progress and how each flag gets completed. | Command | Main Args | Flags | | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | [podman-attach](podman-attach.1.md)
[podman-container-attach](podman-attach.1.md) | running containers | **--detach-keys** -> _ctrl-_ | | [podman-auto-update](podman-auto-update.1.md) | none | | | [podman-build](podman-build.1.md)
[podman-image-build](podman-build.1.md) | default shell | skipping for now because flags are defined in buildah | | [podman-commit](podman-commit.1.md)
[podman-container-commit](podman-commit.1.md) | all containers | **--change** -> _CMD, ENTRYPOINT, ENV, EXPOSE, LABEL, STOPSIGNAL, USER, VOLUME, WORKDIR_
**--format** -> _oci,docker_
**--iidfile** -> default shell
**--author** -> none
**--message** -> none | | [podman-container-checkpoint](podman-container-checkpoint.1.md) | running containers | **--export** -> default shell | | [podman-container-cleanup](podman-container-cleanup.1.md) | exited containers | **--exec** -> none | | [podman-container-exists](podman-container-exists.1.md) | all containers | | | [podman-container-prune](podman-container-prune.1.md) | none | **--filter** -> none | | [podman-container-restore](podman-container-restore.1.md) | all containers | **--import** -> default shell
**--name** -> none | | [podman-container-runlabel](podman-container-runlabel.1.md) | all images | **--authfile** -> default shell

[podman-container-create](podman-create.1.md) | only first arg as image | **--annotation** -> none
**--attach** -> _STDIN,STDOUT,STDERR_
**--authfile** -> default shell
**--blkio-weight** -> _10,100,1000_
**--blkio-weight-device** -> default shell
**--cap-add** -> all linux capabilities see man capabilities.7
**--cap-drop** -> all linux capabilities see man capabilities.7
**--cgroupns** -> _host,container:[name],ns:[path],private_
**--cgroups** -> _enabled,disabled,no-conmon_
**--cgroup-parent** -> default shell
**--cidfile** -> default shell
**--conmon-pidfile** -> default shell
**--cpu\*** -> none
**--detach-keys** -> _ctrl-_

**--device-cgroup-rule** -> none
**--device-read/write\*** -> default shell
**--entrypoint** -> none
**--env** -> none | I will edit this post to keep the table up to date with my progress.

I love the idea of this, and what I've seen of the implementation so far (but it will take me many more hours to complete review). If that zsh PR ever gets merged, then vendored in here, I would love to replace my crufty hand-written zsh completion.

My first showstopper question is: how are you testing this? I fired up a bash shell and:
```console
$ source <(./bin/podman completion bash)
$ podman p<TAB>   --> bash: _get_comp_words_by_ref: command not found
```
[UPDATED TO ADD: bash-5.0.11-2.fc32]
you have to use `./bin/podman p<TAB>` because it implements some custom hidden args on the podman command
I did (try both `podman` and `./bin/podman`); I redacted one just for brevity. No difference.
ok i installed it with `sudo make install` i think the new podman version needs to be in your $PATH

Solved: you need to run this first:
```console
$ source /usr/share/bash-completion/bash_completion
```

Can you find a way to disable the showing of global flags on subcommands? It is the opposite of useful to see 15+ suggestions on a command that has only two flags:
```console
$ podman rmi -<TAB><TAB>
--all                --identity           --runroot=
--cgroup-manager     --identity=          --runtime
--cgroup-manager=    --log-level          --runtime=
--cni-config-dir     --log-level=         --storage-driver
--cni-config-dir=    --namespace          --storage-driver=
--conmon             --namespace=         --storage-opt
--conmon=            --network-cmd-path   --storage-opt=
--events-backend     --network-cmd-path=  --syslog
--events-backend=    --remote             --tmpdir
--force              --remote=            --tmpdir=
--help               --root               -a
--hooks-dir          --root=              -f
--hooks-dir=         --runroot            -r
```

There is a weird inconsistency in the autogenerated flag completions, and I can't figure out where it's happening:
```
    flags+=("--something=")
    two_word_flags+=("--something")
```
Note that the first one has an `=` suffix, the second does not. This produces duplicate completions, as seen in my earlier comment above (`--something` and `--something=`). I think it makes most sense to remove the `=` but as long as you stick with one format I'm OK with it.

Also, is there a way to add option completion to flags? E.g. `--cgroup-manager` currently uses file completion, it should offer only two choices.

And hey, I know I'm griping a lot, but this is really great work. I really like it and think it's much more maintainable than the current bash completions. Thank you for your work on this.

I think thats because these are persistent flags and can therefore used on any subcommand
`podman rmi --log-level info imagename` is completely valid

It may be valid but it's completely useless, and no human will ever want to do that. The possibility is there due to an accident of history. If there is any possible way to remove those from completion, I encourage you to do so.

> There is a weird inconsistency in the autogenerated flag completions, and I can't figure out where it's happening:
> 
> ```
>     flags+=("--something=")
>     two_word_flags+=("--something")
> ```
> 
> Note that the first one has an `=` suffix, the second does not. This produces duplicate completions, as seen in my earlier comment above (`--something` and `--something=`). I think it makes most sense to remove the `=` but as long as you stick with one format I'm OK with it.
> 
Thats how it should be since we can both use `--log-level info` and `--log-level=info`
> Also, is there a way to add option completion to flags? E.g. `--cgroup-manager` currently uses file completion, it should offer only two choices.

Sure stared to implement this already on some flags see: https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/6442#issuecomment-637775222

> It may be valid but it's completely useless, and no human will ever want to do that. The possibility is there due to an accident of history. If there is any possible way to remove those from completion, I encourage you to do so.

I will look into this.

>Thats how it should be since we can both use --log-level info and --log-level=info

Understood, but our target audience are (presumably) people with more than ten minutes of Linux experience, and understand how to use flags. The clutter added by duplicate flag entries is horrendous: try `podman create -<TAB><TAB>`. It is actually evil. There should be one and only one completion convention.

>Sure stared to implement this already on some flags

Oops, I missed that - thank you!

zsh: I suspect this is a bug in cobra itself, not your code: any flag whose default is empty causes a zsh completion error:
```console
zsh$ podman volume create<TAB>
_arguments:comparguments:325: invalid option definition: (*-l *--label)*-l[Set metadata for a volume (default [])]:

zsh$ podman completion zsh | grep -3 'Set metadata for a volume'
function _podman_volume_create {
  _arguments \
    '--driver[Specify volume driver name]:' \
    '(*-l *--label)'{\*-l,\*--label}'[Set metadata for a volume (default [])]:' \
    '(*-o *--opt)'{\*-o,\*--opt}'[Set driver specific options (default [])]:' \
    '--cgroup-manager[Cgroup manager to use ("cgroupfs"|"systemd")]:' \
    '--cni-config-dir[Path of the configuration directory for CNI networks]:' \
```
There are 35 instances of this. One easy solution is to `sed -e 's/\[\]/none/'` or `s/\(\[\|\]\)/\\\1/g`
There is already PR in cobra which should fix the zsh issues. For now i would recommend not to test zsh until it gets merged.

>There is already PR in cobra which should fix the zsh issues

Fair enough. I had looked at https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1070 to see if it addressed this, and not found a clear indication, but it's an enormous PR and it's very likely I missed something. I will not look at zsh again for now.
There its mentioned https://github.com/spf13/cobra/issues/1129

> zsh: I suspect this is a bug in cobra itself, not your code: any flag whose default is empty causes a zsh completion error:
> 
> ```
> zsh$ podman volume create<TAB>
> _arguments:comparguments:325: invalid option definition: (*-l *--label)*-l[Set metadata for a volume (default [])]:
> ```

This has been reported here https://github.com/spf13/cobra/issues/1129
and I can confirm the problem will be fixed with https://github.com/spf13/cobra/issues/1070.

> > Thats how it should be since we can both use --log-level info and --log-level=info
> 
> Understood, but our target audience are (presumably) people with more than ten minutes of Linux experience, and understand how to use flags. The clutter added by duplicate flag entries is horrendous: try `podman create -<TAB><TAB>`. It is actually evil. There should be one and only one completion convention.

I agree with you about not showing both `--flag` and `--flag=`.  I've proposed to remove the `--flag=` form in https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1070 which, if accepted, will affect `zsh` and `fish`.  It will not change things for `bash`, but I have something in the pipeline for `bash`.  For now, I'm not aware of a way for you to remove the `--flag=` form for `bash` without having support from Cobra (unless you grep out the `flags+=("--something=")` everywhere after generating the completion script).

If you could weigh in on the comment below to support this suggested change, it would help get it accepted.  Thanks.
https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1070#discussion_r422680307

I have added the completion for all commands/flags except the build, login and logout flags because their are defined in buildah and containers/common. They could also be implemented here but I would prefer to define them in their repo. There is still much room for improvement in the completion functions.

I know that this has a lot of changes to look through but the biggest part contains only the flags and command definition. The main logic lives in `cmd/podman/common/completion.go`. 

This does not replace the current shell completion scripts. I would recommend to wait for this until https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1146 is merged.
/ok-to-test
@Luap99 Please rebase this PR?  What is the current state?

@Luap99 Sadly it needs another rebase.
@edsantiago @vrothberg @QiWang19 PTAL

OK, done.

Please note that the zsh and bash script are not available in their final form - it would be best to rely on this PR.: https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1146

You're right, I have removed the unused code. A large part of the changes are due to formatting and documentation.

The main advantage is that we can support bash/zsh/fish and powershell completion. This also ensures that we don't miss any flags or commands in completion, as they are automatically added (unless they are hidden).

I also noticed that there is no completion for podman-remote. You can simply do `podman-remote completion bash` with this. It even omits the unsupported flags (e.g. --latest ) but i need to figure out how to complete the remote images,containers,etc...

This would also be useful for windows powershell users. However, the powershell script supports completion only for commands and flags, but not for the arguments (containers, images, etc...). I did not test powershell yet because i dont know how to build the windows client.
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
@Luap99 I believe the upstream changes have been committed; have they been vendored into podman yet? I would really, really like to see this PR incorporated. It is great work.

@edsantiago The zsh changes are merged. Which means that zsh and fish shell completion should be fully usable. The bash completion also works but currently provides flag suggestions with and without the equal form. The generated bash script also has something like 18k lines. That's the reason I temporary replaced the go module with @marckhouzam new bash completion branch. PR: https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1146.
I did this to test his changes and provide him with feedback. I can confirm that this works very well and it even enables support for descriptions in bash. While testing I noticed two bugs: https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1162 and https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1171. The latter one would allow podman --remote to complete remote images/containers/etc.. which would be a great improvement although it also requires a running podman endpoint for the subcommand and flag completion. But this might be something I could change if I figure out how to remote wiring exactly works.

This results in two options:

1. Wait for the bash PR to get merged.
2. Vendor in the current cobra master (there is no new release with the zsh change) and use this PR to make it the default for fish and zsh shell completion and keep using our handwritten bash script until the bash PR lands.
@Luap99 This PR needs a rebase, and has cobra got the fixes to allow us to remove the WIP?

@rhatdan Still waiting on https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1146

However I vendored this PR in for now so you can test this if you want.

## How to test this PR
bash:
`source <(bin/podman completion bash)`
zsh:
`bin/podman completion zsh -f "${fpath[1]}/_podman"` and reload the shell
fish:
`bin/podman completion fish | source`

To disable descriptions use it like this. `source <(bin/podman completion bash --no-desc)`
Make sure you do `bin/podman [TAB]`

## How it works
Cobra creates hidden command called `__complete` which will provide the completion values. The shell completion scripts are static and only call this cobra command with the current arguments. This command then provides the correct completions and the shell script just parses this output.
Full documentation: https://github.com/spf13/cobra/blob/master/shell_completions.md

## Advantages
- All commands and flags are automatically completed (unless there are hidden or deprecated). There is no need to do anything if you add a command or flag.
- The custom completion logic is written in go and there is no need to do this for each shell. Just add a `ValidArgsFunction`. to the command or flag. You can see such functions in `cmd/podman/common/completion.go`.
- Support for the fish shell.
- Eventually support for powershell (not with this PR as this also needs some cobra changes https://github.com/spf13/cobra/pull/1208).
- Full support for `podman-remote` and `podman --remote` including the parsing from `--url` and `--connection` values so that the the correct containers/images from the remote endpoint are completed.
- Fix some currently not working behavior e.g. `podman --log-level info [TAB]`

## TODO
- I have added some FIXMEs which need your attention.
- Flags for `build` do not have custom completion functions yet as there are defined in `buildah`. These flags will default to path completion. They can be easily added in buildah later on. The same goes for `login` and `logout`.

I think this is a great improvement in usability, so I really recommend that you try it.

/cc @edsantiago @mheon @TomSweeneyRedHat @vrothberg

@Luap99 Lets get this into Podman even if we don't abandon the hand built completions right away.  So people can play with it.

Make `podman completions` hidden so we don't have to document and support it for other use cases other then in the completion scripts.
/approve

The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
@baude @edsantiago @mheon @TomSweeneyRedHat PTAL

@Luap99 once this is merged, could you please create a PR in github.com.podman.io and update the install instructions there with a small section on how to use this?  Somewhere under this [Building from scratch](https://podman.io/getting-started/installation#building-from-scratch) section of the doc.

I took a very high level scan, the changes LGTM.  I just don't have time to do a very detailed review at this point.

I continue to think this is super-exciting work, but in testing it right now I notice that flag completion on subcommands is including global options:
```console
$ ./bin/podman ps -<TAB>
--all               -a  -- Show ...   <--good
--cgroup-manager        -- Cgroup ... <--BAD
...
```
Can you configure this so global options are only shown if there's no subcommand?

> Can you configure this so global options are only shown if there's no subcommand?

Well this is not natively supported. Persistent flags are valid in this case and can be used there. This is how most programs use them. I agree with you that this is not really that useful for podman.

The correct way to stop this would be to define the flags as local ones on the root cmd. However this would break backwards compability. Local flags could only be used like this: `podman --log-level info run ...`, `podman run --log-level info ...` would no longer work. I think this is not desirable.

I believe I could hack around this by dropping the unwanted flags on the fly if a subcmd gets completed. I will try this if you really prefer that option.
The undesirable flags don't show up in `podman subcommand --help`, so there must be some internal flag that distinguishes them. Can that be used by the completion code?

> The undesirable flags don't show up in podman subcommand --help, so there must be some internal flag that distinguishes them. Can that be used by the completion code?

Yes I can drop the undesired flags easily but this comes at a cost. `podman ps --log[TAB]` would no longer get completed. I just want to point this out. I agree with you that hiding them could result in a better user experience.

A compromise could be that we show the persistent flags only after at least three chars are typed. That should be enough to not print to much.

> I can drop the undesired flags easily but this comes at a cost. podman ps --log[TAB] would no longer get completed

That's a feature, not a bug! `--log-level` is a global option, not a subcomand option. It should never be visible on subcommands. It is, due to unfortunate backward-compatibility reasons. Maybe some scripts use it, but command-line users (the ones using completion) should be encouraged to use those only at the root level (`podman --log-level=xxx`). 

>show the persistent flags only after at least three chars are typed

I don't think they should be shown at all. (At the subcommand level).
@edsantiago This now works as you wish.
@Luap99 you need to rebase on current master to fix CI. My apologies.
I have opened https://github.com/containers/common/pull/314 to implement the login/logout flag completions.
@Luap99 Needs a rebase.  I would love to finally push this over the line.

@rhatdan Rebased

The last commit still needs a buildah release with the changes in https://github.com/containers/buildah/pull/2656. Or alternatively this could also be merged without the last commit for now as this only adds the completions for the build command.
Ok lets cut a buildah release and move forward

I've been testing this out all afternoon, and it's just beautiful work. I'd really like to see this merged before the tree changes and @Luap99 needs to rebase yet again. @containers/podman-maintainers can we make an effort to review and merge this?
/lgtm

Thought I'd commented on this.  
FWIW, it LGTM, but I'm not at all a subject matter expert here.
Rebased with the new doc format since this would have blocked CI post-merge otherwise.

@Luap99 I don't think it would've blocked, since github would merge on top of master (i.e. rebasing for you) but thanks.

I didn't have a strong urge to repeat my testing this morning, and yesterday was a nice quiet PR day, so I just diff'ed against the previous submission:
```console
$ git cdif pr/6442{-rev05,}
```
...and this is the only change I see which does not look like one of yesterday's PRs:
```diff
diff --git a/cmd/podman/completion/completion.go b/cmd/podman/completion/completion.go
index 71d07082c..ead8d1f05 100644
--- a/cmd/podman/completion/completion.go
+++ b/cmd/podman/completion/completion.go
@@ -87,7 +87,7 @@ func completion(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
        }

        _, err = io.WriteString(w, fmt.Sprintf(
-               "# This file is generated with %q; see: podman-completion(1)\n", cmd.CommandPath(),
+               "\n# This file is generated with %q; see: podman-completion(1)\n", cmd.CommandPath(),
        ))
        return err
 }
```
LGTM!

/lgtm
/hold cancel
